questions.question: What is the primary architectural concept that allows Dask to process a 50GB CSV file on a laptop that only has 16GB of RAM without crashing?
options: Dask automatically upgrades Python's internal memory allocator to 64-bit architecture.
Dask heavily compresses the data in memory using gzip before processing it.

Dask divides the massive dataset into many small Pandas DataFrames (partitions). Using Lazy Evaluation and Task Graphs, it orchestrates the execution to load, process, and discard one partition at a time — keeping total RAM usage under the 16GB limit throughout the computation.
Dask streams the data directly to the GPU for faster processing.
questions.answer: Dask divides the massive dataset into many small Pandas DataFrames (partitions). Using Lazy Evaluation and Task Graphs, it orchestrates the execution to load, process, and discard one partition at a time — keeping total RAM usage under the 16GB limit throughout the computation.
questions.explanation: Dask does not load the entire dataset into memory simultaneously. It pipelines the work so only a tiny fraction of the data is resident in memory at any given millisecond.

questions.question: When you write data manipulation code using Dask (e.g., df.groupby('category').mean()), why does the execution take 0.01 seconds initially?
options: Because Dask uses Lazy Evaluation. It does not execute the data operations immediately. Instead, it instantly constructs a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) detailing the steps required, waiting for you to explicitly call .compute() to begin the actual massive computation.
Because Dask heavily utilizes caching in Redis clusters.
Because Dask ignores complex mathematical operations until they are printed to the console.
Because Dask offloads the processing to a supercomputer in the cloud instantly.
questions.answer: Because Dask uses Lazy Evaluation. It does not execute the data operations immediately. Instead, it instantly constructs a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) detailing the steps required, waiting for you to explicitly call .compute() to begin the actual massive computation.
questions.explanation: Lazy Evaluation is the core concept of both Dask and Apache Spark. Building the mathematical instruction list (the Graph) takes milliseconds; actually moving terabytes of data across CPUs takes hours.

questions.question: If you are designing the backend infrastructure for a distributed Deep Learning training system that requires complex orchestration between specific CPUs and specialized GPUs, which framework is the industry standard choice?
questions.explanation: Ray is structurally designed for general-purpose distributed computing and fine-grained heterogeneous resource management (specifically CPUs vs GPUs), making it the dominant foundation for modern AI and ML distributed workloads.
